title: Introduction and Scope of Pedestrian Crossing Behavior Research

supporting_points: This paper aimed to examine pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), extending it by adding descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency to evaluate their respective impacts on pedestrians' behavior intentions.

theme_refs.label: Structural Equation Modeling (Sem) Based On Theory Of Planned Behavior (Tpb)
theme_refs.synthesis_note: Structural equation modeling (SEM) based on Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) appears in 5 evidence unit(s), with 0 gap signal(s) and 0 contradiction signal(s).

theme_refs.label: Evaluating Contribution Of Tpb Components And Extended Factors
theme_refs.synthesis_note: Evaluating contribution of TPB components and extended factors appears in 5 evidence unit(s), with 0 gap signal(s) and 0 contradiction signal(s).

supporting_points: Instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency are significant predictors of pedestrian violating crossing behavior intention, providing better predictive utility than the basic TPB model (Model 4 achieved best fit: CFI=0.996, RMSEA=0.030)

Descriptive norm is a stronger predictor than subjective norm; when descriptive norm was added, subjective norm became non-significant, indicating that pedestrians' observations of family and friends' behavior have greater influence than perceived approval from them

argument_moves.purpose: Close the section by stating the field-level takeaway rather than repeating paper-by-paper details.

supporting_points: Instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency are significant predictors of pedestrian violating crossing behavior intention, providing better predictive utility than the basic TPB model (Model 4 achieved best fit: CFI=0.996, RMSEA=0.030)
Perceived behavioral control was not a significant predictor in any model, likely because crossing the street was not perceived as challenging for the sample population

theme_refs.synthesis_note: Evaluating contribution of TPB components and extended factors appears in 5 evidence unit(s), with 0 gap signal(s) and 0 contradiction signal(s).

supporting_points: This study investigates how mobile phone distractions (music distraction, phone conversation distraction, and text distraction) affect the crossing behavior and visual attention allocation of pedestrians at a signalized intersection. The research addresses the growing concern about pedestrian safety as mobile phone usage while crossing has increased globally, with approximately one-third of pedestrians in China and 60% of adults in the US reportedly using mobile phones while crossing streets.

argument_moves.purpose: Anchor the section in 6 extracted claim signal(s) and the strongest matrix evidence.

theme_refs.synthesis_note: Evaluating contribution of TPB components and extended factors appears in 5 evidence unit(s), with 0 gap signal(s) and 0 contradiction signal(s).

supporting_points: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrians' crossing performance, followed by phone conversation distraction, with music distraction having the least impact

Instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency are significant predictors of pedestrian violating crossing behavior intention, providing better predictive utility than the basic TPB model (Model 4 achieved best fit: CFI=0.996, RMSEA=0.030)

supporting_points: This study investigates how mobile phone distractions (music distraction, phone conversation distraction, and text distraction) affect the crossing behavior and visual attention allocation of pedestrians at a signalized intersection. The research addresses the growing concern about pedestrian safety as mobile phone usage while crossing has increased globally, with approximately one-third of pedestrians in China and 60% of adults in the US reportedly using mobile phones while crossing streets.

theme_refs.label: Structural Equation Modeling (Sem) Based On Theory Of Planned Behavior (Tpb)
theme_refs.synthesis_note: Structural equation modeling (SEM) based on Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) appears in 5 evidence unit(s), with 0 gap signal(s) and 0 contradiction signal(s).

supporting_points: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrians' crossing performance, followed by phone conversation distraction, with music distraction having the least impact

Music distraction causes pedestrians to initiate crossing later (1.28s vs 0.69s), increase pupil diameter (2.86mm vs 2.75mm), and reduce scanning frequency (6.00 vs 6.82) and fixation points toward traffic signal areas

argument_moves.purpose: Close the section by stating the field-level takeaway rather than repeating paper-by-paper details.

theme_refs.label: Evaluating Contribution Of Tpb Components And Extended Factors
theme_refs.synthesis_note: Evaluating contribution of TPB components and extended factors appears in 5 evidence unit(s), with 0 gap signal(s) and 0 contradiction signal(s).

supporting_points: This paper aimed to examine pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), extending it by adding descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency to evaluate their respective impacts on pedestrians' behavior intentions.

argument_moves.purpose: Anchor the section in 6 extracted claim signal(s) and the strongest matrix evidence.

theme_refs.label: Evaluating Contribution Of Tpb Components And Extended Factors
theme_refs.synthesis_note: Evaluating contribution of TPB components and extended factors appears in 5 evidence unit(s), with 0 gap signal(s) and 0 contradiction signal(s).

supporting_points: Instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency are significant predictors of pedestrian violating crossing behavior intention, providing better predictive utility than the basic TPB model (Model 4 achieved best fit: CFI=0.996, RMSEA=0.030)

Descriptive norm is a stronger predictor than subjective norm; when descriptive norm was added, subjective norm became non-significant, indicating that pedestrians' observations of family and friends' behavior have greater influence than perceived approval from them

argument_moves.purpose: Close the section by stating the field-level takeaway rather than repeating paper-by-paper details.
